source: NIST CFReDS 2015 Data Leakage Case (cfreds.nist.gov/data_leakage_case/data-leakage-case.html). Step-by-step answer key available at NIST. Enriched from VIGIA-REAL-002.

attack_chains.name: Coordination & Planning
attack_chains.summary: Email communication between Sr. Informant (iaman.informant@nist.gov) and conspirator (spy.conspirator@nist.gov) coordinating data requests and exfiltration plan.

attack_chains.name: Data Discovery & Collection
attack_chains.summary: Suspect searches for and identifies sensitive corporate files, copies them to staging locations and removable media.

attack_chains.name: Exfiltration
attack_chains.summary: Data exfiltrated via personal email (iaman.informant.personal@gmail.com), web services, and removable USB media. Files renamed to evade detection.

attack_chains.name: Anti-Forensics & Cover-Up
attack_chains.summary: Installation and use of cleanup tools to destroy evidence of data access and exfiltration. File renaming and deletion to hide tracks.

findings.description: Email correspondence between iaman.informant@nist.gov and spy.conspirator@nist.gov discussing data requests
findings.notes: Coordination emails establish conspiracy. Look in Outlook PST/OST, email client databases, or webmail cache. Shows planning and intent.

findings.description: Personal Gmail account iaman.informant.personal@gmail.com used for data exfiltration
findings.notes: Personal email used to send corporate data outside the organization. Check browser history, email client config, sent items. T1567 - Exfiltration Over Web Service.

findings.description: Evidence of file searches for sensitive corporate data on the workstation
findings.notes: Check Windows Search history (WordWheelQuery in NTUSER.DAT), Explorer TypedPaths, Recent docs. Shows suspect actively sought out sensitive files.

findings.description: Sensitive files copied to removable USB media (rm#3)
findings.notes: USN Journal, shellbags, LNK files, or MFT entries showing file copy operations to removable drive. Cross-reference with USB device connection artifacts (SYSTEM\MountedDevices, setupapi.dev.log). T1048 exfiltration channel.

findings.description: Files renamed with misleading extensions or names to evade detection during exfiltration
findings.notes: File rename operations visible in USN Journal or MFT $FN vs $SI. Renamed files to disguise data type/content. T1564 - Hide Artifacts.

findings.description: Anti-forensic cleanup tool installed and executed on the system
findings.notes: CCleaner or similar cleanup tool. Check prefetch files, program installation artifacts (Uninstall registry keys), Amcache. T1070 - Indicator Removal on Host.

findings.description: USB removable device connected to workstation (device serial number and connection timestamps)
findings.notes: USBSTOR registry entries, setupapi.dev.log first/last connection times. Links the removable media evidence (rm#3) to the suspect's PC. Essential for chain of custody.

findings.description: Work email account iaman.informant@nist.gov identifies the suspect user
findings.notes: Suspect's work identity. Found in email client configuration, Outlook profile, or account settings. Links workstation activity to the named insider.

findings.description: Network activity from suspect IP 10.11.11.128 showing data transfer
findings.notes: VIGIA key_iocs. Suspect's IP. May appear in network connection logs, browser history URLs, or SRUM network usage data.

findings.description: PC workstation IP 10.11.11.129 identified in authentication or network logs
findings.notes: VIGIA key_iocs. PC IP from auth_log context. May correlate with logon events or network shares accessed.

findings.description: LNK files or Recent documents showing access to sensitive corporate files
findings.notes: Windows .lnk shortcut files in Recent folder preserve timestamps and paths of accessed files even after originals are deleted. Strong evidence of what was accessed and when.

findings.description: Browser history showing access to personal email or cloud storage services for exfiltration
findings.notes: Web browser artifacts (Chrome History db, IE WebCacheV01.dat, Firefox places.sqlite) showing Gmail access, cloud uploads. Timestamps correlate with data collection phase.

findings.description: Evidence of file deletion or wiping activity post-exfiltration
findings.notes: USN Journal deletion entries, $Recycle.Bin artifacts, or cleanup tool logs showing deliberate destruction of evidence after exfiltration completed.

findings.description: Timeline correlation shows coherent sequence: request → search → copy → rename → exfiltrate → cleanup
findings.notes: Super-timeline analysis (plaso/log2timeline) showing the attack sequence compressed into a suspicious timeframe. Supports intent/planning interpretation over coincidental activity.
